title: Valid Anagram
description:
Given two strings `s` and `t`, return `true` if `t` is an anagram of `s`, and `false` otherwise.

An **Anagram** is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase, typically using all the original letters exactly once.
whatShouldHappen: Check if two strings have the same characters with the same frequencies
Return true if they are anagrams, false otherwise
skeletonHint: First, check if the lengths are equal. Then, use a frequency map (object) to count characters in the first string and decrement counts for characters in the second string.
patternExplanation: Comparing frequency maps of characters is an O(n) approach to check if one string is a permutation of another.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
description: Given a string `s`, find the length of the **longest substring** without repeating characters.
whatShouldHappen: Identify the longest contiguous sequence of unique characters
Return the length of this substring
Handle empty strings and strings with all identical characters
skeletonHint: Use a sliding window with two pointers (`left` and `right`). Expand the window by moving `right`, and if you hit a duplicate character, shrink the window from the `left` until the duplicate is removed.
patternExplanation: The sliding window pattern is ideal here because we are looking for a contiguous range (substring) that satisfies a specific condition (no repeating characters).

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
description:
Given a string `s`, return the longest **palindromic substring** in `s`.

A string is palindromic if it reads the same forward and backward.
whatShouldHappen: Identify the longest contiguous sequence that is a palindrome
Return the substring itself, not just its length
Handle single character strings and strings with multiple palindromes of the same length
skeletonHint: Consider every character (and the gap between characters) as a potential center of a palindrome. Expand outwards from the center as long as the characters match.
patternExplanation: Expanding around the center avoids the O(n³) brute force approach and allows us to find palindromes in O(n²) time by checking all 2n-1 potential centers.

title: Valid Parentheses
description:
Given a string `s` containing just the characters `'('`, `')'`, `'{'`, `'}'`, `'['` and `']'`, determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:
1. Open brackets must be closed by the same type of brackets.
2. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
3. Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.
whatShouldHappen: Use a stack to keep track of opening brackets
Match closing brackets with the top of the stack
Ensure the stack is empty at the end
skeletonHint: Use a stack to store opening brackets. When you encounter a closing bracket, check if it matches the bracket at the top of the stack. If it doesn't, or if the stack is empty, the string is invalid.
patternExplanation: A stack is perfect for this problem because it follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, which matches the nested nature of parentheses.

title: Group Anagrams
description:
Given an array of strings `strs`, group the **anagrams** together. You can return the answer in **any order**.

An **Anagram** is a word or phrase formed by rearranging the letters of a different word or phrase, typically using all the original letters exactly once.
whatShouldHappen: Group strings that contain the exact same characters
Use a sorted version of the string or a frequency count as a key in a map
Return an array of arrays, where each inner array contains grouped anagrams
skeletonHint: Anagrams will have the same characters. If you sort the characters of each string, all anagrams will result in the same sorted string. Use this sorted string as a key in a Hash Map to group the original strings.
patternExplanation: A Hash Map allows us to group items by a common property (in this case, the sorted character sequence) in O(N * K log K) time, where N is the number of strings and K is the maximum length of a string.

title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
description:
You are given a string `s` and an integer `k`. You can choose any character of the string and change it to any other uppercase English character. You can perform this operation at most `k` times.

Return the length of the longest substring containing the same letter you can get after performing the above operations.
whatShouldHappen: Maintain a sliding window and count character frequencies within it
Identify the most frequent character in the current window
Shrink the window if the number of characters to replace (window size - max frequency) exceeds k
Return the maximum window size achieved
skeletonHint: Use a sliding window. Keep track of the frequency of characters in the window and the count of the most frequent character. The condition `windowLength - maxFrequency <= k` must hold.
patternExplanation: The sliding window allows us to find the maximum range where we can replace at most 'k' characters to make all characters in the range the same.

title: Minimum Window Substring
description:
Given two strings `s` and `t` of lengths `m` and `n` respectively, return the **minimum window substring** of `s` such that every character in `t` (including duplicates) is included in the window. If there is no such substring, return the empty string `""`.

The testcases will be generated such that the answer is **unique**.
whatShouldHappen: Use a sliding window to find substrings containing all characters of t
Shrink the window from the left to find the smallest valid substring
Return the shortest such substring found
skeletonHint: Use two pointers for a sliding window. Maintain a frequency map for string `t` and another for the current window. Expand `right` until the window is valid, then contract `left` to minimize the window.
patternExplanation: The sliding window is the most efficient way to find a substring meeting specific constraints by expanding and contracting pointers in O(n) time.

title: Valid Palindrome
description:
A phrase is a **palindrome** if, after converting all uppercase letters into lowercase letters and removing all non-alphanumeric characters, it reads the same forward and backward. Alphanumeric characters include letters and numbers.

Given a string `s`, return `true` if it is a **palindrome**, or `false` otherwise.
whatShouldHappen: Clean the string by removing non-alphanumeric characters and lowercasing
Use two pointers (start and end) to compare characters
Return true if all characters match, false otherwise

traceTable.inputLabel: s = 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'

skeletonHint: Use two pointers, one starting at the beginning and one at the end. Move them towards each other, skipping any non-alphanumeric characters. Compare the characters at each step (case-insensitive).

input: A man, a plan, a canal: Panama

patternExplanation: The two-pointer technique allows us to compare elements from both ends of the string efficiently without extra space.

title: Palindromic Substrings
description:
Given a string `s`, return the number of **palindromic substrings** in it.

A string is a **palindrome** when it reads the same backward as forward.

A **substring** is a contiguous sequence of characters within the string.
whatShouldHappen: Identify all possible palindromic substrings
Count both odd-length and even-length palindromes
Return the total count
skeletonHint: Each character and each gap between characters can be the center of a palindrome. Expand outwards from each center as long as the characters match.
patternExplanation: Expansion from center allows us to find all palindromes by checking each potential center in O(n²) time, which is more efficient than checking all O(n²) substrings.
